blocks.content:
Sometimes a program needs to choose between more than two options.

In previous topics we used `if` and `else`. This allowed the program to choose between two actions.

But sometimes a program needs to check several possible conditions.

For these situations we use an `else if` chain.

An `else if` chain allows the program to check conditions one by one.

The program starts with the first condition. If it is false, the program checks the next one.

The checks continue from top to bottom until one of the conditions becomes true.

When the program finds the first true condition, it runs that block of code and stops checking the rest.

`int score = 75;`

A variable named `score` is created with the value 75.

`if (score > 90)`

The program first checks the first condition.

It asks: is the score greater than 90?

75 is not greater than 90, so the condition is false.

The program moves to the next condition.

`else if (score > 70)`

Now the program checks the next condition.

It asks: is the score greater than 70?

75 is greater than 70, so the condition is true.

The program runs the code inside this block:

`Console.WriteLine("Good");`

After this, the program stops checking the remaining conditions because a matching condition has already been found.
Retype the code from the example and check it.

Imagine a program that checks the total price of a purchase in a store.

Create a variable `total` and set its value to 80.

If the total is greater than 100, print "10% discount".

If the total is greater than 50, print "5% discount".

Otherwise print "No discount".

Use `if`, `else if`, and `else`.

Conditions in an `else if` chain are checked from top to bottom.

When the program finds the first true condition, it runs that block and stops checking the rest.
